title: Renal Cell Carcinoma
oneLiner: Renal Cell Carcinoma (RCC) is a hypervascular kidney cancer often found incidentally; classic triad is rare, but paraneoplastic syndromes clue you in.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**
A 62-year-old man with a 40-pack-year smoking history presents with painless gross hematuria and dull left flank pain. He has lost 8 kg over 3 months, and on examination you palpate a firm, non-tender mass in the left upper quadrant. His BP is 160/95 (new hypertension), and CBC shows polycythemia (Hb 18 g/dL) with normal platelets.

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- **Painless gross hematuria** – classic sign of any renal tract malignancy; blood from the tumor or invading collecting system.
- **Dull flank pain** – stretching of the renal capsule or invasion of perinephric tissues.
- **Palpable flank mass** – advanced disease (often >5 cm); suggests extension beyond kidney.
- **Weight loss** – cachexia from chronic inflammation or advanced/metastatic disease.
- **Smoking** – strongest modifiable risk factor (2–3× increased risk).
- **New hypertension** – possible renin secretion by tumor or compression of renal artery.
- **Polycythemia** – a paraneoplastic phenomenon from ectopic erythropoietin (EPO) production by clear cell RCC.
- **Age 60s** – peak incidence 60–70 years; male predominance (2:1).

**Atypical presentations (trickers):**
- **Incidentaloma** – asymptomatic patient gets a CT for back pain or liver workup; a solid enhancing renal mass is found. Most common presentation today (∼60%).
- **Varicocele (left-sided, new onset)** – left testicular vein drains into left renal vein; RCC obstructing the left renal vein causes acute left varicocele that does not decompress when supine.
- **Fever of unknown origin** – RCC can secrete pyrogenic cytokines (IL-6); pure fever without other s/sx – often mistaken for infection until imaging.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
RCC is a malignant tumor arising from the renal tubular epithelium. It accounts for ∼85% of all kidney cancers and is notorious for its unpredictable behaviour – some grow slowly, others metastasise early.

**Why it matters:**
- **Frequency:** 9th most common cancer in men; incidence increasing due to incidental detection.
- **Stakes:** 5-year survival for localised disease is >80%, but if metastatic (25-30% at presentation) it drops to <15%. Early detection saves lives.
- **Common pitfalls:** Misdiagnosing a complex renal cyst as benign; failing to recognise paraneoplastic syndromes as clues; assuming a solid renal mass is always RCC (could be oncocytoma or angiomyolipoma).
- **Treatment evolution:** Surgery is curative for localised disease; advanced disease now uses targeted therapies (TKI, mTOR inhibitors) and immunotherapy, but resistance develops – making early diagnosis critical.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story:**
Most RCC arises from the proximal convoluted tubule. In clear cell RCC (∼75% of cases), the initiating event is loss of function of the **VHL tumour suppressor gene** (on chromosome 3p). This is either hereditary (von Hippel-Lindau disease) or acquired (sporadic mutation plus second hit). VHL protein normally targets **HIF (hypoxia-inducible factor)** for degradation. Without functional VHL, HIF accumulates even in the presence of oxygen, causing a "pseudo-hypoxic" state. HIF then upregulates pro-angiogenic factors (especially **VEGF**, PDGF) and growth factors (TGF-α, EPO). The result: a hypervascular tumour with prominent capillary networks – visible on CT as intense enhancement.

**Mechanism → Clinical sign:**
- **Hematuria:** Tumour erodes into the collecting system or renal pelvis.
- **Flank mass / pain:** Capsular stretching (pain) or large bulky tumour (mass).
- **Polycythemia:** Ectopic EPO production by tumour cells.
- **Hypercalcemia:** Ectopic PTHrP (parathyroid hormone-related protein) – not bone metastases initially.
- **Hypertension:** Renin secretion or compression of renal artery (activation of RAAS).
- **Weight loss / cachexia:** Elevated IL-6, TNF-alpha.
- **Varicocele:** Left renal vein compression by tumor → increased venous pressure in left testicular vein.
- **Fever:** Tumour necrosis or cytokine production.

**Connecting to pattern recognition:**
- The classic triad (hematuria, flank pain, mass) indicates advanced disease because all three require significant tumour burden.
- Paraneoplastic syndromes often appear earlier than the mass – if you see unexplained polycythemia or hypercalcemia in an older smoker, think RCC.
- Incidental detection: CT shows a solid enhancing mass; the enhancement reflects the hypervascular nature driven by VEGF.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** | Most cases are sporadic without clear family history | No risk factors identified; patient just has RCC |
| **Lifestyle** | **Cigarette smoking** (most common modifiable) | "40-pack-year smoker" or "current smoker" |
| **Obesity** | Increased estrogen/adipokines; BMI >30 | "Obese female, hypertensive" |
| **Genetic / Hereditary** | von Hippel-Lindau (VHL) disease (autosomal dominant, 3p deletion) – clear cell RCC, often bilateral/multifocal, <40 years | "Young patient with hemangioblastoma of retina/CNS, pheochromocytoma, or family history of RCC" |
| **Hereditary** | Hereditary papillary RCC (MET mutations) | "Papillary type tumour, young age" |
| **Hypertension** | Independently associated (mechanism unclear) | "Long-standing hypertension, maybe on diuretics" |
| **Occupational** | Exposure to trichloroethylene, cadmium, asbestos | "Works in dry-cleaning or battery factory" |
| **Drug-related** | Long-term use of **phenacetin** (now withdrawn), **diuretics** (weak), **analgesic nephropathy** | "Headache medication abuse history" |
| **End-stage renal disease / acquired cystic disease** | Long-term dialysis → cysts → increased RCC risk | "Dialysis for 10 years, multiple cysts" |

**Most common cause:** Cigarette smoking (attributable risk ∼30%). Always ask about smoking history.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:**
No single blood test – diagnosis is by imaging. Suspicion arises from ultrasound/CT. The gold standard is **triphasic CT abdomen** (non-contrast, arterial, portal venous). Features of malignant renal mass: **solid, enhancing >15-20 Hounsfield units, irregular margins, necrosis/calcification, invasion of perinephric fat or renal vein**. Biopsy is rarely needed before surgery unless the mass is suspicious for lymphoma, metastasis, or abscess.

**Investigations table:**
| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|------------------|----------------|
| **CT abdomen with contrast (triphasic)** | Solid enhancing mass; can see fat (angiomyolipoma – negative HU), or simple cyst (water density, no enhancement) | To confirm RCC, stage (TNM), assess renal vein/IVC thrombus | Enhancement >20 HU = malignant until proven otherwise; non-enhancing = cyst. |
| **Renal ultrasound** | Simple cyst vs solid mass; can detect renal vein thrombus | First-line for hematuria or flank pain; helps differentiate cyst | Simple cyst (anechoic, thin wall, posterior enhancement) = benign; complex cysts (septations, solid components) need CT. |
| **MRI abdomen** | Better soft tissue contrast; characterisation of complex cysts, IVC thrombus | When CT contraindicated (allergy, renal impairment) or to evaluate venous extension | Same as CT – enhancement = solid tumour. |
| **Chest CT** | Lung metastases (most common site) | Staging for all newly diagnosed RCC | Multiple nodules – metastatic disease. |
| **Bone scan** | Bone metastases (lytic, blastic) | If bone pain, elevated ALP, or symptoms | Hot spots; but RCC bone mets are often lytic, so sensitivity lower than PET. |
| **PET-CT** | Not routine – limited role in RCC; can show metastases | In certain atypical cases or for recurrence | RCC can be FDG-avid, but many are variable – not a substitute for CT. |
| **FBC** | Polycythemia (Hb >17 g/dL), normocytic anaemia | Baseline; paraneoplastic clues | Hb elevated → think clear cell with EPO production. |
| **Urea/Creatinine** | Assess renal function | Essential before contrast + surgery | eGFR >30 (contrast acceptable with precautions). |
| **LFTs** | Raised ALP (bone mets or cholestasis); normal bilirubin but high ALT/AST (liver mets) | Baseline + monitor for metastases | Isolated ALP elevation with normal GGT – think bone mets. |
| **Calcium (corrected)** | Hypercalcemia (PTHrP) | Paraneoplastic screening | Corrected Ca >2.6 mmol/L – investigate for PTHrP. |
| **Urinalysis** | Microscopic hematuria | Confirm blood, exclude infection | >3 RBC/hpf – further imaging needed. |
| **Renal mass biopsy** | Histology: clear cell, papillary, chromophobe, oncocytic | **Rarely** – only if imaging atypical (lymphoma, abscess, metastasis) or to plan neoadjuvant therapy | Core needle biopsy has high accuracy. |

**Single finding that confirms:**
- CT: a solid, enhancing renal mass without benign fat (no negative HU). That is RCC until proven otherwise.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour):**
- If **acute severe hematuria with clot retention** → urgent bladder washout, catheterise, IV fluids; call urology on-call for possible angioembolisation if unstable.
- If **suspected renal vein/IVC thrombus causing pulmonary embolism** → urgent CT chest + anticoagulation (LMWH; therapeutic dose: e.g., enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg SC once daily). Consider IVC filter if thrombus extends above hepatic veins.
- **Stabilise hypercalcemia** (if present): IV 0.9% NaCl 200 mL/h, then calcitonin/bisphosphonate (pamidronate 60-90 mg IV over 2h) if refractory.
- **No specific emergency** for localised RCC – but do not delay imaging.

**Ward (24-72 hours):**
- **Staging:** CT chest + triphasic CT abdomen (if not done) + bloods (FBC, U&E, LFT, Ca, ALP). If metastatic, consider biopsy to confirm histology.
- **Discuss with urology MDT:**
  - Localised (T1a, <4 cm, favorable anatomy) → **partial nephrectomy** (nephron-sparing) – best for renal function and cardiovascular outcomes.
  - Localised (T1b-T2, >4 cm, or central location) → **radical nephrectomy** (open or laparoscopic). For T2 in elderly, consider active surveillance.
  - Locally advanced (T3-T4, or venous thrombus) → **radical nephrectomy + thrombectomy**; may need vascular surgery involvement.
- **Risk stratification:** Use **UCLA Integrated Staging System (UISS)** or **Leibovich score** to predict recurrence – guides surveillance.
- **Patients unfit for surgery:** Consider **ablation** (cryoablation, radiofrequency ablation) for small tumours (<3 cm), or **active surveillance** (slow-growing RCC in elderly).

**Long-term:**
- **Localised disease (N0M0):** Post-nephrectomy surveillance: CT chest/abdomen every 3-6 months for 3 years, then annually to 5 years. No adjuvant therapy unless high risk (e.g., clear cell with sarcomatoid features) – then discuss sunitinib or pembrolizumab in trials.
- **Metastatic disease:**
  - **First-line:** **Tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI)** – e.g., sunitinib 50 mg PO once daily for 4 weeks on/2 weeks off; or **pazopanib** 800 mg PO once daily. Monitor blood pressure (hypertension common), liver enzymes, hypothyroidism.
  - **Alternatively (if high PD-L1 expression):** Combination **pembrolizumab + axitinib** (immunotherapy + TKI) – improves OS.
  - **Poor risk (IMDC criteria):** **Temsirolimus** 25 mg IV weekly (mTOR inhibitor).
  - **After progression:** Second-line options: cabozantinib, nivolumab (anti-PD-1), lenvatinib + everolimus.
- **High-dose IL-2** – used in selected patients (good performance status, clear cell, lung metastases only) – can produce durable complete responses but with severe toxicity.
- **Palliative radiotherapy** for painful bone metastases or brain metastases (whole brain or SRS).
- **Palliative nephrectomy** for symptomatic primary (pain, bleeding) even in metastatic setting – but only if the patient has good performance status.

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**
- **Do not** perform a needle biopsy on a simple renal cyst – it is benign. Misinterpreting a Bosniak 2F cyst (few thin septations) as malignant.
- **Do not** use a partial nephrectomy for T3 tumour – you will not get clear margins.
- **Do not** start anticoagulation for a small renal vein thrombus that is asymptomatic and non-occlusive – wait for MDT.
- **Do not** give systemic therapy for a solitary metastasis – consider metastasectomy (good outcomes for single lung/pancreas lesion).
- **Do not** ignore new-onset hypertension in a patient on sunitinib – it is a common side effect but also a marker of efficacy; still treat with antihypertensives (caution with CYP3A4 inhibitors).
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Renal Cell Carcinoma | Angiomyolipoma (AML) | Oncocytoma | Renal Cell Cyst (Bosniak 4) | Transitional Cell Carcinoma (TCC) of renal pelvis |
|---------|----------------------|-----------------------|------------|-----------------------------|---------------------------------------------------|
| **Age/Sex** | 60s, male > female | 40-60s, female > male | 60s, male | Any age, but 50-70 | 60-80, male > female, smoking |
| **Pain** | Dull flank, may be ache | Usually asymptomatic; can cause retroperitoneal bleed (Wunderlich syndrome – acute severe pain) | Usually none | None unless infected | Colicky pain (obstructing clot) |
| **Hematuria** | Painless gross (common) | Uncommon unless large | Rare | Rare | Visible/painless common; often with flank pain |
| **Palpable mass** | Possible in advanced | Occasionally large | Rare (large) | Not unless huge (simple) | Not unless obstructed kidney |
| **Imaging hallmark** | Solid, enhancing >20 HU, heterogeneous, irregular margins; can have calcifications | **Fat density (≤ -10 HU)** – pathognomonic; may be solid with macroscopic fat | Homogeneous, central stellate scar, ± spoke-wheel enhancement pattern; **no necrosis** | Thick irregular wall + enhancing nodules (Bosniak 4); malignant potential | Hypovascular filling defect in renal pelvis; **not solid mass in renal parenchyma** |
| **Key differential point** | Enhancement after contrast; no fat | **Fat on CT or MRI** – benign (no malignant potential) | **Central scar**; no gross necrosis; difficult to distinguish from RCC on CT – often requires biopsy | Cystic morphology with solid elements – requires cyst classification | Location – within the collecting system; not in the cortex/medulla |
| **Management** | Nephrectomy or active surveillance | If >4 cm and high risk of bleeding: embolisation or resection; otherwise observe | If proven by biopsy: can follow (very low metastasis risk); often resected because imaging cannot reliably exclude RCC | Surgical excision (partial nephrectomy if T1a, else radical) | Nephroureterectomy (entire ureter + bladder cuff); TCC is aggressive, high recurrence |
| **Prognosis** | Stage-dependent (see above) | Excellent (no malignant potential) | Excellent | Good if caught early; worse if sarcomatoid | Poorer than RCC stage for stage due to recurrence in urinary tract |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
- **Metastatic dissemination (most feared):** Lung (cough, hemoptysis), bone (pain, fractures, cord compression), brain (headache, seizures), liver (abnormal LFTs, jaundice). If untreated, 5-year survival <15%. Early detection of single organ metastasis may allow cure with metastasectomy.
- **Renal vein / IVC thrombus:** Presents with acute scrotal varicocele, leg swelling (if IVC occlusion), or pulmonary embolism (chest pain, dyspnea). If thrombus is small, it can be resected en bloc with nephrectomy; if massive (supradiaphragmatic) requires cardiopulmonary bypass. **Late presentation: bilateral leg edema, ascites (IVC occlusion) → emergency thrombectomy or stenting.**
- **Paraneoplastic hypercalcemia:** Presents with nausea, vomiting, confusion, polyuria, bone pain. If missed → dehydration, acute kidney injury, coma. Treat with aggressive IV fluids + bisphosphonate.
- **Rupture/bleeding (Wunderlich syndrome):** Sudden severe flank pain, hypotension, palpable mass. CT shows perinephric hematoma. Needs angioembolisation or emergency nephrectomy if unstable.
- **Renal impairment:** After bilateral nephrectomy (rare) or from tumor infiltration. If both kidneys involved (e.g., VHL) → dialysis dependence. Mitigate with nephron-sparing surgery.
- **Infection of necrotic tumour:** Fever, sepsis, positive blood cultures – treat with broad-spectrum antibiotics + urgent nephrectomy if source control needed.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonic for paraneoplastic syndromes of RCC (classic: Stauffer's is liver dysfunction):**
**"SHEEP"**
- **S** – Stauffer's syndrome (cholestatic liver dysfunction without metastases) – reversible with nephrectomy
- **H** – Hypercalcemia (PTHrP)
- **E** – Erythrocytosis / polycythemia (EPO)
- **E** – Erythrocytes – also anemia of chronic disease
- **P** – Pyrexia (fever), PTHrP, Proteinuria (also hypertension due to renin)

**Mnemonic for risk factors:**
**"RCC: Risk factors — SMOKE CALV"**
- **S** – Smoking
- **M** – Male
- **O** – Obesity
- **K** – K (kidney disease: ESKD, acquired cystic disease)
- **E** – Environmental (trichloroethylene, cadmium)
- **C** – Cystic (acquired)
- **A** – Antihypertensives? (hypertension itself)
- **L** – Lynch syndrome (Hereditary non-polyposis colorectal cancer – also increases RCC)
- **V** – VHL

**Clinical pearls:**
1. **Incidentaloma is the most common presentation.** If you see a solid, enhancing renal mass on a CT done for another reason, assume RCC until proven otherwise.
2. **Left-sided varicocele that does not reduce when supine = suspect RCC with renal vein thrombus.** Work up with CT venogram.
3. **Polycythemia + flank mass = RCC until proven otherwise.** Do not attribute to COPD or sleep apnoea without imaging.
4. **New-onset hypertension in a patient over 60** – consider RCC as a cause (especially if unilateral flank pain). Check renal ultrasound.
5. **Weight loss + hematuria in a smoker** – high risk of advanced RCC. Get imaging as an emergency.
6. **RCC can cause fever of unknown origin** – persistent low-grade fever without focus; order CT abdomen if FUO workup negative.
7. **Tumour thrombus extension into IVC** – RCC is one of the few cancers that can do this. If you see an IVC mass on CT, always think RCC (also can be liver cancer or adrenal). Ask about flank pain and hematuria.

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**
- **Mistake:** Calling a Bosniak 2F cyst malignant and referring for surgery. **Fix:** Follow Bosniak guidelines – 2F requires surveillance, not resection.
- **Mistake:** Forgetting to check renin/aldosterone/EPO in a patient with resistant hypertension + polycythemia. **Fix:** Check renal ultrasound before antihypertensive changes.
- **Mistake:** Starting anticoagulation for deep vein thrombosis without considering RCC as the underlying cause. **Fix:** If unprovoked DVT + flank pain – get CT.
- **Mistake:** Missing hypercalcemia until patient is confused. **Fix:** Request Ca on any patient with weight loss and bone pain.
- **Mistake:** Assuming a negative urine dipstick for blood rules out RCC. **Fix:** Microscopic hematuria can be intermittent; any episode of visible hematuria warrants imaging regardless of dipstick.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**
- **NEWS2 ≥ 7** (especially due to hypotension, tachycardia, or low SpO2) – possible massive PE from IVC thrombus or tumour rupture with hemorrhage. Escalate to ICU.
- **Lactate > 4 mmol/L** – septic shock from infected necrotic tumour, or hypoperfusion from haemorrhage.
- **GCS < 13** – hypercalcemic crisis (Ca > 3.5 mmol/L) or brain metastasis with raised ICP. Call senior and ICU.
- **Visible frank bleeding from renal bed post-op (nephrectomy)** – immediate return to theatre; call senior surgeon, order crossmatch.
- **Acute kidney injury (AKI) stage 2 or higher** in a patient with solitary kidney after partial nephrectomy – call nephrology/urology.
- **Hypercalcemia with confusion or ECG changes** (short QT interval, prolonged QRS, bradycardia): give IV fluids, calcitonin, call senior.
- **Suspected spinal cord compression** (new back pain + leg weakness, bladder/bowel dysfunction) – emergency MRI spine, call oncology/radiotherapy.

**Specific thresholds (follow local protocol):**
| Parameter | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| Heart rate | >130 bpm | Check for hypovolaemia/PE; senior review |
| Systolic BP | <90 mmHg (or drop >40 from baseline) | IV fluids, consider pressors, ICU |
| SpO2 | <88% on room air | Oxygen, CT pulmonary angiogram if PE suspected |
| Urine output | <0.5 mL/kg/h for 2 hours | Fluid challenge, check U&E, call senior |
| GCS | drop ≥2 points | CT head (brain mets), check Ca, ABG |
| Lactate | >4 mmol/L | Sepsis protocol, fluids, senior |
| Corrected Ca | >3.0 mmol/L | IV fluids + bisphosphonate; >3.5 → ICU |
| Hb drop | >2 g/dL post-op or active bleeding | Crossmatch, surgical review |
| Cr rise | >1.5× baseline in 24h | Stop nephrotoxins, hold immunotherapy (pembrolizumab can cause acute interstitial nephritis) |

## Renal Cell Carcinoma
**One-liner:** Renal Cell Carcinoma (RCC) is a hypervascular kidney cancer often found incidentally; classic triad is rare, but paraneoplastic syndromes clue you in.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See
**Classic vignette:**
A 62-year-old man with a 40-pack-year smoking history presents with painless gross hematuria and dull left flank pain. He has lost 8 kg over 3 months, and on examination you palpate a firm, non-tender mass in the left upper quadrant. His BP is 160/95 (new hypertension), and CBC shows polycythemia (Hb 18 g/dL) with normal platelets.

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- **Painless gross hematuria** – classic sign of any renal tract malignancy; blood from the tumor or invading collecting system.
- **Dull flank pain** – stretching of the renal capsule or invasion of perinephric tissues.
- **Palpable flank mass** – advanced disease (often >5 cm); suggests extension beyond kidney.
- **Weight loss** – cachexia from chronic inflammation or advanced/metastatic disease.
- **Smoking** – strongest modifiable risk factor (2–3× increased risk).
- **New hypertension** – possible renin secretion by tumor or compression of renal artery.
- **Polycythemia** – a paraneoplastic phenomenon from ectopic erythropoietin (EPO) production by clear cell RCC.
- **Age 60s** – peak incidence 60–70 years; male predominance (2:1).

**Atypical presentations (trickers):**
- **Incidentaloma** – asymptomatic patient gets a CT for back pain or liver workup; a solid enhancing renal mass is found. Most common presentation today (∼60%).
- **Varicocele (left-sided, new onset)** – left testicular vein drains into left renal vein; RCC obstructing the left renal vein causes acute left varicocele that does not decompress when supine.
- **Fever of unknown origin** – RCC can secrete pyrogenic cytokines (IL-6); pure fever without other s/sx – often mistaken for infection until imaging.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)
RCC is a malignant tumor arising from the renal tubular epithelium. It accounts for ∼85% of all kidney cancers and is notorious for its unpredictable behaviour – some grow slowly, others metastasise early.

**Why it matters:**
- **Frequency:** 9th most common cancer in men; incidence increasing due to incidental detection.
- **Stakes:** 5-year survival for localised disease is >80%, but if metastatic (25-30% at presentation) it drops to <15%. Early detection saves lives.
- **Common pitfalls:** Misdiagnosing a complex renal cyst as benign; failing to recognise paraneoplastic syndromes as clues; assuming a solid renal mass is always RCC (could be oncocytoma or angiomyolipoma).
- **Treatment evolution:** Surgery is curative for localised disease; advanced disease now uses targeted therapies (TKI, mTOR inhibitors) and immunotherapy, but resistance develops – making early diagnosis critical.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings
**The story:**
Most RCC arises from the proximal convoluted tubule. In clear cell RCC (∼75% of cases), the initiating event is loss of function of the **VHL tumour suppressor gene** (on chromosome 3p). This is either hereditary (von Hippel-Lindau disease) or acquired (sporadic mutation plus second hit). VHL protein normally targets **HIF (hypoxia-inducible factor)** for degradation. Without functional VHL, HIF accumulates even in the presence of oxygen, causing a "pseudo-hypoxic" state. HIF then upregulates pro-angiogenic factors (especially **VEGF**, PDGF) and growth factors (TGF-α, EPO). The result: a hypervascular tumour with prominent capillary networks – visible on CT as intense enhancement.

**Mechanism → Clinical sign:**
- **Hematuria:** Tumour erodes into the collecting system or renal pelvis.
- **Flank mass / pain:** Capsular stretching (pain) or large bulky tumour (mass).
- **Polycythemia:** Ectopic EPO production by tumour cells.
- **Hypercalcemia:** Ectopic PTHrP (parathyroid hormone-related protein) – not bone metastases initially.
- **Hypertension:** Renin secretion or compression of renal artery (activation of RAAS).
- **Weight loss / cachexia:** Elevated IL-6, TNF-alpha.
- **Varicocele:** Left renal vein compression by tumor → increased venous pressure in left testicular vein.
- **Fever:** Tumour necrosis or cytokine production.

**Connecting to pattern recognition:**
- The classic triad (hematuria, flank pain, mass) indicates advanced disease because all three require significant tumour burden.
- Paraneoplastic syndromes often appear earlier than the mass – if you see unexplained polycythemia or hypercalcemia in an older smoker, think RCC.
- Incidental detection: CT shows a solid enhancing mass; the enhancement reflects the hypervascular nature driven by VEGF.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)
| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** | Most cases are sporadic without clear family history | No risk factors identified; patient just has RCC |
| **Lifestyle** | **Cigarette smoking** (most common modifiable) | "40-pack-year smoker" or "current smoker" |
| **Obesity** | Increased estrogen/adipokines; BMI >30 | "Obese female, hypertensive" |
| **Genetic / Hereditary** | von Hippel-Lindau (VHL) disease (autosomal dominant, 3p deletion) – clear cell RCC, often bilateral/multifocal, <40 years | "Young patient with hemangioblastoma of retina/CNS, pheochromocytoma, or family history of RCC" |
| **Hereditary** | Hereditary papillary RCC (MET mutations) | "Papillary type tumour, young age" |
| **Hypertension** | Independently associated (mechanism unclear) | "Long-standing hypertension, maybe on diuretics" |
| **Occupational** | Exposure to trichloroethylene, cadmium, asbestos | "Works in dry-cleaning or battery factory" |
| **Drug-related** | Long-term use of **phenacetin** (now withdrawn), **diuretics** (weak), **analgesic nephropathy** | "Headache medication abuse history" |
| **End-stage renal disease / acquired cystic disease** | Long-term dialysis → cysts → increased RCC risk | "Dialysis for 10 years, multiple cysts" |

**Most common cause:** Cigarette smoking (attributable risk ∼30%). Always ask about smoking history.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means
**Diagnostic criteria:**
No single blood test – diagnosis is by imaging. Suspicion arises from ultrasound/CT. The gold standard is **triphasic CT abdomen** (non-contrast, arterial, portal venous). Features of malignant renal mass: **solid, enhancing >15-20 Hounsfield units, irregular margins, necrosis/calcification, invasion of perinephric fat or renal vein**. Biopsy is rarely needed before surgery unless the mass is suspicious for lymphoma, metastasis, or abscess.

**Investigations table:**
| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|------------------|----------------|
| **CT abdomen with contrast (triphasic)** | Solid enhancing mass; can see fat (angiomyolipoma – negative HU), or simple cyst (water density, no enhancement) | To confirm RCC, stage (TNM), assess renal vein/IVC thrombus | Enhancement >20 HU = malignant until proven otherwise; non-enhancing = cyst. |
| **Renal ultrasound** | Simple cyst vs solid mass; can detect renal vein thrombus | First-line for hematuria or flank pain; helps differentiate cyst | Simple cyst (anechoic, thin wall, posterior enhancement) = benign; complex cysts (septations, solid components) need CT. |
| **MRI abdomen** | Better soft tissue contrast; characterisation of complex cysts, IVC thrombus | When CT contraindicated (allergy, renal impairment) or to evaluate venous extension | Same as CT – enhancement = solid tumour. |
| **Chest CT** | Lung metastases (most common site) | Staging for all newly diagnosed RCC | Multiple nodules – metastatic disease. |
| **Bone scan** | Bone metastases (lytic, blastic) | If bone pain, elevated ALP, or symptoms | Hot spots; but RCC bone mets are often lytic, so sensitivity lower than PET. |
| **PET-CT** | Not routine – limited role in RCC; can show metastases | In certain atypical cases or for recurrence | RCC can be FDG-avid, but many are variable – not a substitute for CT. |
| **FBC** | Polycythemia (Hb >17 g/dL), normocytic anaemia | Baseline; paraneoplastic clues | Hb elevated → think clear cell with EPO production. |
| **Urea/Creatinine** | Assess renal function | Essential before contrast + surgery | eGFR >30 (contrast acceptable with precautions). |
| **LFTs** | Raised ALP (bone mets or cholestasis); normal bilirubin but high ALT/AST (liver mets) | Baseline + monitor for metastases | Isolated ALP elevation with normal GGT – think bone mets. |
| **Calcium (corrected)** | Hypercalcemia (PTHrP) | Paraneoplastic screening | Corrected Ca >2.6 mmol/L – investigate for PTHrP. |
| **Urinalysis** | Microscopic hematuria | Confirm blood, exclude infection | >3 RBC/hpf – further imaging needed. |
| **Renal mass biopsy** | Histology: clear cell, papillary, chromophobe, oncocytic | **Rarely** – only if imaging atypical (lymphoma, abscess, metastasis) or to plan neoadjuvant therapy | Core needle biopsy has high accuracy. |

**Single finding that confirms:**
- CT: a solid, enhancing renal mass without benign fat (no negative HU). That is RCC until proven otherwise.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning
**Immediate (first hour):**
- If **acute severe hematuria with clot retention** → urgent bladder washout, catheterise, IV fluids; call urology on-call for possible angioembolisation if unstable.
- If **suspected renal vein/IVC thrombus causing pulmonary embolism** → urgent CT chest + anticoagulation (LMWH; therapeutic dose: e.g., enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg SC once daily). Consider IVC filter if thrombus extends above hepatic veins.
- **Stabilise hypercalcemia** (if present): IV 0.9% NaCl 200 mL/h, then calcitonin/bisphosphonate (pamidronate 60-90 mg IV over 2h) if refractory.
- **No specific emergency** for localised RCC – but do not delay imaging.

**Ward (24-72 hours):**
- **Staging:** CT chest + triphasic CT abdomen (if not done) + bloods (FBC, U&E, LFT, Ca, ALP). If metastatic, consider biopsy to confirm histology.
- **Discuss with urology MDT:**
  - Localised (T1a, <4 cm, favorable anatomy) → **partial nephrectomy** (nephron-sparing) – best for renal function and cardiovascular outcomes.
  - Localised (T1b-T2, >4 cm, or central location) → **radical nephrectomy** (open or laparoscopic). For T2 in elderly, consider active surveillance.
  - Locally advanced (T3-T4, or venous thrombus) → **radical nephrectomy + thrombectomy**; may need vascular surgery involvement.
- **Risk stratification:** Use **UCLA Integrated Staging System (UISS)** or **Leibovich score** to predict recurrence – guides surveillance.
- **Patients unfit for surgery:** Consider **ablation** (cryoablation, radiofrequency ablation) for small tumours (<3 cm), or **active surveillance** (slow-growing RCC in elderly).

**Long-term:**
- **Localised disease (N0M0):** Post-nephrectomy surveillance: CT chest/abdomen every 3-6 months for 3 years, then annually to 5 years. No adjuvant therapy unless high risk (e.g., clear cell with sarcomatoid features) – then discuss sunitinib or pembrolizumab in trials.
- **Metastatic disease:**
  - **First-line:** **Tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI)** – e.g., sunitinib 50 mg PO once daily for 4 weeks on/2 weeks off; or **pazopanib** 800 mg PO once daily. Monitor blood pressure (hypertension common), liver enzymes, hypothyroidism.
  - **Alternatively (if high PD-L1 expression):** Combination **pembrolizumab + axitinib** (immunotherapy + TKI) – improves OS.
  - **Poor risk (IMDC criteria):** **Temsirolimus** 25 mg IV weekly (mTOR inhibitor).
  - **After progression:** Second-line options: cabozantinib, nivolumab (anti-PD-1), lenvatinib + everolimus.
- **High-dose IL-2** – used in selected patients (good performance status, clear cell, lung metastases only) – can produce durable complete responses but with severe toxicity.
- **Palliative radiotherapy** for painful bone metastases or brain metastases (whole brain or SRS).
- **Palliative nephrectomy** for symptomatic primary (pain, bleeding) even in metastatic setting – but only if the patient has good performance status.

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**
- **Do not** perform a needle biopsy on a simple renal cyst – it is benign. Misinterpreting a Bosniak 2F cyst (few thin septations) as malignant.
- **Do not** use a partial nephrectomy for T3 tumour – you will not get clear margins.
- **Do not** start anticoagulation for a small renal vein thrombus that is asymptomatic and non-occlusive – wait for MDT.
- **Do not** give systemic therapy for a solitary metastasis – consider metastasectomy (good outcomes for single lung/pancreas lesion).
- **Do not** ignore new-onset hypertension in a patient on sunitinib – it is a common side effect but also a marker of efficacy; still treat with antihypertensives (caution with CYP3A4 inhibitors).

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table
| Feature | Renal Cell Carcinoma | Angiomyolipoma (AML) | Oncocytoma | Renal Cell Cyst (Bosniak 4) | Transitional Cell Carcinoma (TCC) of renal pelvis |
|---------|----------------------|-----------------------|------------|-----------------------------|---------------------------------------------------|
| **Age/Sex** | 60s, male > female | 40-60s, female > male | 60s, male | Any age, but 50-70 | 60-80, male > female, smoking |
| **Pain** | Dull flank, may be ache | Usually asymptomatic; can cause retroperitoneal bleed (Wunderlich syndrome – acute severe pain) | Usually none | None unless infected | Colicky pain (obstructing clot) |
| **Hematuria** | Painless gross (common) | Uncommon unless large | Rare | Rare | Visible/painless common; often with flank pain |
| **Palpable mass** | Possible in advanced | Occasionally large | Rare (large) | Not unless huge (simple) | Not unless obstructed kidney |
| **Imaging hallmark** | Solid, enhancing >20 HU, heterogeneous, irregular margins; can have calcifications | **Fat density (≤ -10 HU)** – pathognomonic; may be solid with macroscopic fat | Homogeneous, central stellate scar, ± spoke-wheel enhancement pattern; **no necrosis** | Thick irregular wall + enhancing nodules (Bosniak 4); malignant potential | Hypovascular filling defect in renal pelvis; **not solid mass in renal parenchyma** |
| **Key differential point** | Enhancement after contrast; no fat | **Fat on CT or MRI** – benign (no malignant potential) | **Central scar**; no gross necrosis; difficult to distinguish from RCC on CT – often requires biopsy | Cystic morphology with solid elements – requires cyst classification | Location – within the collecting system; not in the cortex/medulla |
| **Management** | Nephrectomy or active surveillance | If >4 cm and high risk of bleeding: embolisation or resection; otherwise observe | If proven by biopsy: can follow (very low metastasis risk); often resected because imaging cannot reliably exclude RCC | Surgical excision (partial nephrectomy if T1a, else radical) | Nephroureterectomy (entire ureter + bladder cuff); TCC is aggressive, high recurrence |
| **Prognosis** | Stage-dependent (see above) | Excellent (no malignant potential) | Excellent | Good if caught early; worse if sarcomatoid | Poorer than RCC stage for stage due to recurrence in urinary tract |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?
- **Metastatic dissemination (most feared):** Lung (cough, hemoptysis), bone (pain, fractures, cord compression), brain (headache, seizures), liver (abnormal LFTs, jaundice). If untreated, 5-year survival <15%. Early detection of single organ metastasis may allow cure with metastasectomy.
- **Renal vein / IVC thrombus:** Presents with acute scrotal varicocele, leg swelling (if IVC occlusion), or pulmonary embolism (chest pain, dyspnea). If thrombus is small, it can be resected en bloc with nephrectomy; if massive (supradiaphragmatic) requires cardiopulmonary bypass. **Late presentation: bilateral leg edema, ascites (IVC occlusion) → emergency thrombectomy or stenting.**
- **Paraneoplastic hypercalcemia:** Presents with nausea, vomiting, confusion, polyuria, bone pain. If missed → dehydration, acute kidney injury, coma. Treat with aggressive IV fluids + bisphosphonate.
- **Rupture/bleeding (Wunderlich syndrome):** Sudden severe flank pain, hypotension, palpable mass. CT shows perinephric hematoma. Needs angioembolisation or emergency nephrectomy if unstable.
- **Renal impairment:** After bilateral nephrectomy (rare) or from tumor infiltration. If both kidneys involved (e.g., VHL) → dialysis dependence. Mitigate with nephron-sparing surgery.
- **Infection of necrotic tumour:** Fever, sepsis, positive blood cultures – treat with broad-spectrum antibiotics + urgent nephrectomy if source control needed.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls
**Mnemonic for paraneoplastic syndromes of RCC (classic: Stauffer's is liver dysfunction):**
**"SHEEP"**
- **S** – Stauffer's syndrome (cholestatic liver dysfunction without metastases) – reversible with nephrectomy
- **H** – Hypercalcemia (PTHrP)
- **E** – Erythrocytosis / polycythemia (EPO)
- **E** – Erythrocytes – also anemia of chronic disease
- **P** – Pyrexia (fever), PTHrP, Proteinuria (also hypertension due to renin)

**Mnemonic for risk factors:**
**"RCC: Risk factors — SMOKE CALV"**
- **S** – Smoking
- **M** – Male
- **O** – Obesity
- **K** – K (kidney disease: ESKD, acquired cystic disease)
- **E** – Environmental (trichloroethylene, cadmium)
- **C** – Cystic (acquired)
- **A** – Antihypertensives? (hypertension itself)
- **L** – Lynch syndrome (Hereditary non-polyposis colorectal cancer – also increases RCC)
- **V** – VHL

**Clinical pearls:**
1. **Incidentaloma is the most common presentation.** If you see a solid, enhancing renal mass on a CT done for another reason, assume RCC until proven otherwise.
2. **Left-sided varicocele that does not reduce when supine = suspect RCC with renal vein thrombus.** Work up with CT venogram.
3. **Polycythemia + flank mass = RCC until proven otherwise.** Do not attribute to COPD or sleep apnoea without imaging.
4. **New-onset hypertension in a patient over 60** – consider RCC as a cause (especially if unilateral flank pain). Check renal ultrasound.
5. **Weight loss + hematuria in a smoker** – high risk of advanced RCC. Get imaging as an emergency.
6. **RCC can cause fever of unknown origin** – persistent low-grade fever without focus; order CT abdomen if FUO workup negative.
7. **Tumour thrombus extension into IVC** – RCC is one of the few cancers that can do this. If you see an IVC mass on CT, always think RCC (also can be liver cancer or adrenal). Ask about flank pain and hematuria.

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**
- **Mistake:** Calling a Bosniak 2F cyst malignant and referring for surgery. **Fix:** Follow Bosniak guidelines – 2F requires surveillance, not resection.
- **Mistake:** Forgetting to check renin/aldosterone/EPO in a patient with resistant hypertension + polycythemia. **Fix:** Check renal ultrasound before antihypertensive changes.
- **Mistake:** Starting anticoagulation for deep vein thrombosis without considering RCC as the underlying cause. **Fix:** If unprovoked DVT + flank pain – get CT.
- **Mistake:** Missing hypercalcemia until patient is confused. **Fix:** Request Ca on any patient with weight loss and bone pain.
- **Mistake:** Assuming a negative urine dipstick for blood rules out RCC. **Fix:** Microscopic hematuria can be intermittent; any episode of visible hematuria warrants imaging regardless of dipstick.

### 10. Escalation Criteria
**When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**
- **NEWS2 ≥ 7** (especially due to hypotension, tachycardia, or low SpO2) – possible massive PE from IVC thrombus or tumour rupture with hemorrhage. Escalate to ICU.
- **Lactate > 4 mmol/L** – septic shock from infected necrotic tumour, or hypoperfusion from haemorrhage.
- **GCS < 13** – hypercalcemic crisis (Ca > 3.5 mmol/L) or brain metastasis with raised ICP. Call senior and ICU.
- **Visible frank bleeding from renal bed post-op (nephrectomy)** – immediate return to theatre; call senior surgeon, order crossmatch.
- **Acute kidney injury (AKI) stage 2 or higher** in a patient with solitary kidney after partial nephrectomy – call nephrology/urology.
- **Hypercalcemia with confusion or ECG changes** (short QT interval, prolonged QRS, bradycardia): give IV fluids, calcitonin, call senior.
- **Suspected spinal cord compression** (new back pain + leg weakness, bladder/bowel dysfunction) – emergency MRI spine, call oncology/radiotherapy.

**Specific thresholds (follow local protocol):**
| Parameter | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| Heart rate | >130 bpm | Check for hypovolaemia/PE; senior review |
| Systolic BP | <90 mmHg (or drop >40 from baseline) | IV fluids, consider pressors, ICU |
| SpO2 | <88% on room air | Oxygen, CT pulmonary angiogram if PE suspected |
| Urine output | <0.5 mL/kg/h for 2 hours | Fluid challenge, check U&E, call senior |
| GCS | drop ≥2 points | CT head (brain mets), check Ca, ABG |
| Lactate | >4 mmol/L | Sepsis protocol, fluids, senior |
| Corrected Ca | >3.0 mmol/L | IV fluids + bisphosphonate; >3.5 → ICU |
| Hb drop | >2 g/dL post-op or active bleeding | Crossmatch, surgical review |
| Cr rise | >1.5× baseline in 24h | Stop nephrotoxins, hold immunotherapy (pembrolizumab can cause acute interstitial nephritis) |
